title: System Enumeration: z/OS Pentest Recon
description: Adapt the mainframed/ENUM_REFACTORED tool into a hands-on walkthrough. Enumerate APF libraries, RACF settings, SVC table, TSO authorization tables, logged-on users, and system version info. Based on Soldier of FORTRAN's z/OS Enumeration Pentesting Tool.

steps.title: Enumeration Overview
steps.screen:
z/OS System Enumeration — Pentest Recon

Source: mainframed/pentesting/ENUM_REFACTORED
Author: Soldier of FORTRAN (Philip Young)

This lab adapts the REXX enumeration tool into
interactive TSO commands you can run on TK5.

What we'll enumerate:
  1. OS version, JES, VTAM info
  2. Security manager (RACF) configuration
  3. APF-authorized libraries
  4. TSO authorization tables (AUTHCMD/AUTHPGM)
  5. Logged-on users and address spaces
  6. Dataset concatenations (SYSPROC/SYSEXEC)
  7. Master catalog
  8. TESTAUTH authorization check

All of this runs under a NORMAL user account.
No special privileges needed for most checks.
This is what an attacker sees after getting creds.
steps.expected: Understand what system information is available to a normal TSO user.
steps.hint: Most of this info is exposed through TSO commands and REXX STORAGE() function. The system assumes if you're logged on, you're authorized to see system configuration.

steps.title: Step 1: Connect and Login
steps.screen:
Logon ===> HERC01
 IKJ56710I ENTER CURRENT PASSWORD
 ********
 READY

 First recon step: identify yourself.
 TSO PROFILE shows your current settings.
 LISTUSER shows your RACF profile.
steps.expected: Log in as HERC01 and reach TSO READY.
steps.hint: HERC01 / CUL8TR — PF3 to exit ISPF if it auto-starts.

steps.title: Step 2: OS Version and Environment
steps.screen:
READY
 TIME
 IKJ56650I TIME-09:42:31 AM. CPU-00:00:01 SERVICE-2847 SESSION-00:02:14
 READY
 STATUS
 IKJ56650I JOB HERC01(TSU00025) EXECUTING
 READY

 From the ENUM tool, version info comes from:
   CVT (Communications Vector Table) at address 16
   Product name: MVS/SE2 (or z/OS on modern systems)
   TSO version: from CVT+156 → TSVT
   JES version: from CVT+296 → JESCT

 On TK5: MVS 3.8j SP 1.3.2, JES2, VTAM
steps.expected: Run TIME and STATUS to identify the current environment.
steps.hint: TIME shows CPU time and session duration. STATUS shows your job name and execution status. On z/OS, the REXX ENUM tool reads these from control blocks directly.

steps.title: Step 3: RACF Security Manager Info
steps.screen:
READY
 LU HERC01
 USER=HERC01  NAME=HERCULES ADMIN  OWNER=SYS1
  DEFAULT-GROUP=SYS1 PASSDATE=25.054 PASS-INTERVAL=60
  ATTRIBUTES=SPECIAL OPERATIONS
  ...
 READY

 ENUM_REFACTORED checks:
   CVT+992 → RCVT (RACF CVT)
   First 4 bytes = 'RCVT' → RACF is the security manager
   (Could also be 'RTSS' for Top Secret, or ACF2)

 RACF SETROPTS info from RCVT:
   - PROTECT-ALL setting
   - ERASE-ON-SCRATCH setting
   - Password rules (min length, history, revoke count)
   - Audit settings (what gets logged)
   - UADS dataset location
steps.expected: Run LU (LISTUSER) to see your RACF profile. Note SPECIAL and OPERATIONS attributes.
steps.hint: LU HERC01 — SPECIAL means full RACF admin, OPERATIONS means access to all datasets. On TK5, HERC01 already has both. On a real system, finding a user with SPECIAL is the goal.

steps.title: Step 4: APF Library Enumeration
steps.screen:
READY
 LISTALC STATUS
 --DDNAME   DISP
   STEPLIB
            KEEP   SYS1.CMDLIB
   SYSPROC
            KEEP   SYSC.CMDPROC
   SYSEXEC
            KEEP   SYSC.EXEC
   ISPLLIB
            KEEP   ISP.V2R2M0.ISPLOAD
 READY

 APF libraries (from SYS1.PARMLIB(IEAAPF00)):
   SYS1.LINKLIB  — system default, always APF
   SYS1.SVCLIB   — SVC routines
   SYS1.CMDLIB   — TSO commands
   SYSC.LINKLIB  — community software

 The ENUM tool reads the APF table from:
   CVT+484 → CVTAUTHL (static APF list)
   or CVT+140 → ECVT → CSVT (dynamic APF list)

 Key question: Can we UPDATE any of these?
steps.expected: Run LISTALC STATUS to see your allocated datasets. Then check APF library access.
steps.hint: LISTALC STATUS shows what's allocated to your session. To see APF libraries, check SYS1.PARMLIB(IEAAPF00) — or on z/OS, use D PROG,APF operator command.

steps.title: Step 5: Check APF Library Write Access
steps.screen:
READY
 LISTDS 'SYSC.LINKLIB'
 SYSC.LINKLIB
 --RECFM-LRECL-BLKSIZE-DSORG
   U     0     19069   PO
 --VOLUMES--
   PUB001
 READY

 If HERC01 has UPDATE access to an APF library:
   → APF privilege escalation is possible
   → MODESET → supervisor state → ACEE modification
   → SPECIAL + OPERATIONS = full system control

 On TK5 with OPERATIONS attribute:
   HERC01 can write to almost everything.
   This is finding #1 in every assessment.
steps.expected: Check if HERC01 can write to APF libraries like SYSC.LINKLIB.
steps.hint: LISTDS 'SYSC.LINKLIB' shows the dataset attributes. With OPERATIONS authority, HERC01 has implicit UPDATE access to all datasets — the APF privesc path is wide open.

steps.title: Step 6: TSO Authorization Tables
steps.screen:
The ENUM tool reads TSO auth tables from:
   CVT → TSVT (+X'9C') → TPVT (+X'4C') → CTLT (+X'14')

 Tables found:
   IKJEFTE2 / AUTHCMD  — authorized TSO commands
     Commands that require APF authorization:
     ACCOUNT, SYNC, CANCEL, SEND, TRANSMIT, ...

   IKJEFTE8 / AUTHPGM  — authorized programs
     Programs that can run APF-authorized:
     IKJEFT01, IRXJCL, ISRPCP, ...

   IKJEFTNS / NOTBKGND — not-in-background commands
     Commands that cannot run in batch

   IKJEFTAP / AUTHTSF  — authorized TSO service facility

 Why this matters:
   If you can modify these tables (via SYS1.PARMLIB),
   you can add your own programs to the auth list
   → another path to authorized execution
steps.expected: Understand the TSO authorization tables and why they matter for privesc.
steps.hint: The PARMLIB members IKJTSOxx define which commands/programs get APF authorization. Modifying these is another privilege escalation vector — add your program to AUTHPGM.

steps.title: Step 7: Logged-On Users
steps.screen:
READY
 STATUS
 IKJ56650I JOB HERC01(TSU00025) EXECUTING
 READY

 The ENUM tool walks the ASVT (Address Space Vector Table)
 to find all active address spaces:

   CVT+556 → ASVT+512 → ASVT entries
   For each entry → ASCB → check type:
     X'01' = TSO user
     X'02' = Started task
     X'03' = Batch job
     X'04' = System

 On TK5 you'll see:
   **** Started Tasks ****
   JES2     - +MASTER+
   VTAM     - +MASTER+
   NET      - +MASTER+
   TSO      - +MASTER+

   **** TSO Users ****
   HERC01   - HERC01

 Knowing who's logged on helps plan attack timing.
steps.expected: Understand how the ENUM tool discovers active users and address spaces.
steps.hint: On TK5, use STATUS to see your session. On z/OS, the ENUM REXX tool reads ASVT directly from memory using STORAGE() — no special authority needed.

steps.title: Step 8: Master Catalog
steps.screen:
READY
 LISTCAT
 IN-CAT --- SYS1.VMASTCAT
   ...
 READY

 The ENUM tool reads the master catalog name from:
   CVT+140 → ECVT+392 → IPA+224

 Master catalog: SYS1.VMASTCAT

 Why this matters:
   The master catalog is the root of the dataset namespace
   It contains aliases to user catalogs
   Controlling the catalog = controlling dataset resolution
   On TK5: SYS1.VMASTCAT on volume MVSRES

 LISTCAT shows all cataloged datasets.
 LISTCAT UCAT shows user catalogs.
 Each user catalog is a trust boundary.
steps.expected: Identify the master catalog and understand its role in dataset resolution.
steps.hint: LISTCAT without arguments shows entries in the master catalog. The master catalog is the root of trust for dataset name resolution — controlling it means controlling which datasets get found.

steps.title: Lab Complete
steps.screen:
System Enumeration Lab Complete

What we enumerated (as a normal user):
  - OS version and environment
  - RACF security manager configuration
  - APF-authorized libraries + write access
  - TSO authorization tables (AUTHCMD/AUTHPGM)
  - Active users and address spaces
  - Dataset concatenations and search paths
  - Master catalog and dataset namespace

Key findings:
  F1: Identity info exposed via LU command
  F2: APF libraries visible + writable
  F3: TSO auth tables reveal trusted programs
  F4: All address spaces visible to any user
  F5: Master catalog readable by default

Remediation:
  1. Restrict LU to self only (SETROPTS NOLIST)
  2. Audit APF library access profiles
  3. Protect SYS1.PARMLIB members
  4. Enable SMF logging for sensitive commands
  5. Review default RACF UACC settings

Source: mainframed/pentesting/ENUM_REFACTORED
Credits: Soldier of FORTRAN, Mark Zelden, CBT Tape
steps.expected: Review all enumeration findings and remediation steps.
steps.hint: The key takeaway: a normal user account exposes enormous amounts of system configuration. The mainframe security model assumes authenticated users are trusted — this assumption is the root cause.
